howItWorks.intro: Battery bank sizing works backward from your loads. You start with the watt-hours you burn in a day, multiply by the number of cloudy days you want to ride out, then divide by your system voltage and how deeply you are willing to drain the pack. The calculator turns those four numbers into a target capacity in kilowatt-hours and amp-hours, plus a rough cost and cycle-life estimate for the chemistry you pick.
rules: Total Capacity = (Daily Usage × Autonomy Days) ÷ Depth of Discharge
Battery Cost = Capacity (kWh) × Cost per kWh
LiFePO4: 5000+ cycles at 80% DOD; Lead-acid: 1000 at 50% DOD
howItWorks.outro: Chemistry prices and cycle ratings drift as the lithium market moves, and real autonomy depends on your latitude and how many gray-sky days your site actually gets. Treat the output as a planning target and confirm against your panels' winter production and your charge controller's limits before you buy cells.

faq.question: How many days of autonomy should I plan for?
faq.answer: Autonomy is how long the bank runs your loads with zero solar input. For a weekend van or RV, 1–2 days is usually enough since you move and recharge often. A full-time off-grid cabin in a sunny climate typically wants 2–3 days; in a cloudy northern region, plan 3–5 days or add a generator. More autonomy means a bigger, pricier bank, so most builds settle at 2–3 days and cover the rare long storm with a backup generator instead of oversizing batteries.

faq.question: Lithium (LiFePO4) vs lead-acid — how does depth of discharge change the math?
faq.answer: Depth of discharge (DoD) is the fraction of rated capacity you actually use. LiFePO4 tolerates 80–100% DoD, so a 100 Ah lithium battery gives you 80–100 usable Ah. Lead-acid (AGM or flooded) should only go to about 50% DoD to protect cycle life, so a 100 Ah lead battery gives roughly 50 usable Ah. That means lead-acid banks must be sized nearly double the rated capacity of lithium for the same usable energy — a big reason lithium wins on total lifetime cost despite the higher sticker price.

faq.question: What system voltage should I use — 12 V, 24 V, or 48 V?
faq.answer: Higher voltage moves the same power at lower current, which means thinner, cheaper wiring and less heat loss. 12 V suits small van and RV builds under about 1,500 W of load. 24 V is a good middle ground for larger vans and small cabins. 48 V is the standard for whole-home off-grid systems because it keeps amperage manageable — a 5,000 W load draws roughly 417 A at 12 V but only about 104 A at 48 V. Higher voltage also gives you more amp-hours for the same energy, since Ah = Wh ÷ voltage.

faq.question: How do I turn watt-hours into the amp-hours I actually shop for?
faq.answer: Batteries are sold in amp-hours at a given voltage, so convert with Ah = Wh ÷ system voltage. Take your required usable energy in watt-hours, then divide by 12, 24, or 48. For example, 4,000 usable Wh at 48 V is about 83 Ah, while the same 4,000 Wh at 12 V is 333 Ah. Same energy, very different amp-hour number — which is why comparing batteries only makes sense once you have fixed your system voltage.

faq.question: Does the calculator account for inverter and wiring losses?
faq.answer: The core formula sizes the raw battery bank, but real systems lose 10–20% to inverter inefficiency, wiring resistance, and charge/discharge losses. A practical habit is to add roughly 20% headroom on top of the calculated capacity, or enter your daily usage as measured at the loads and pad it. Also leave margin for future loads — off-grid energy use almost always grows after you move in.

faq.question: Is the Off-Grid Battery Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Everything runs in your browser, so you can re-run different voltages, autonomy days, and chemistries as many times as you want while you compare bank designs.

howWeCalculate.intro: The calculator applies the standard off-grid bank-sizing equation, then converts the result into the amp-hours and cost figures you use when shopping for cells.
steps: Multiply your daily energy usage by days of autonomy to get the total energy the bank must store between charges.
Divide by depth of discharge so the usable-energy target reflects how deeply your chemistry can safely drain — 0.80 for LiFePO4, 0.50 for lead-acid.
Convert that capacity to amp-hours by dividing watt-hours by your system voltage (12, 24, or 48 V).
Multiply the kilowatt-hour capacity by the per-kWh price of your chosen chemistry to estimate bank cost, and map DoD to a cycle-life expectation.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These figures are planning estimates. Winter solar production, temperature derating, and inverter losses all shift real requirements, so confirm your final bank size against your panel array and charge controller before purchasing.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this calculator whenever you are sizing or re-sizing a battery bank for a solar system that has to stand on its own without grid backup.
scenarios: Designing an off-grid cabin bank: pin down daily kWh from your appliances, choose 2–3 days autonomy for cloudy stretches, and size a 48 V LiFePO4 pack.
Building a van or RV solar setup: work at 12 V or 24 V, keep autonomy to 1–2 days since you recharge on the move, and check the amp-hours fit your battery box.
Planning a backup power bank: size storage to carry critical loads through outages and compare lithium against cheaper lead-acid on usable capacity.
Comparing chemistries: run the same loads through LiFePO4 at 80% DoD and lead-acid at 50% DoD to see how much extra rated capacity lead-acid demands.
Choosing a system voltage: test 12 V, 24 V, and 48 V to see how amperage and amp-hour requirements shift before you commit to wiring and an inverter.

sections.title: What Is the Off-Grid Battery Calculator?
sections.body: The Off-Grid Battery Calculator sizes the battery bank a solar system needs to run without grid backup. You enter the energy you burn in a day, how many cloudy days you want to survive without sun, how deeply your battery chemistry can safely discharge, and your system voltage. It returns the total storage capacity you need in kilowatt-hours, the amp-hour figure you actually shop for, a rough cost for the chemistry you pick, and an expected cycle life. It is built for cabins, vans, RVs, and backup power builds where undersizing means the lights go out and oversizing wastes thousands of dollars.

sections.title: The Formula, Worked Through
sections.body: Bank capacity in amp-hours equals daily watt-hours × days of autonomy ÷ (system voltage × depth of discharge). Say a small cabin uses 2,000 Wh per day and you want 2 days of autonomy on a 24 V system with a lead-acid pack limited to 50% depth of discharge. That is 2,000 × 2 ÷ (24 × 0.5) ≈ 333 Ah. Swap to LiFePO4 at 80% DoD and the same loads need only 2,000 × 2 ÷ (24 × 0.8) ≈ 208 Ah — the deeper safe discharge shrinks the pack by more than a third. The math is simple; the payoff is that every variable is one you can measure or choose deliberately.

sections.title: Depth of Discharge Drives the Whole Design
sections.body: Depth of discharge is the single number that separates a lithium build from a lead-acid one. LiFePO4 cells shrug off 80–100% discharge cycle after cycle, so nearly all their rated capacity is usable. Flooded and AGM lead-acid batteries need you to stop around 50% to reach their rated cycle life, so you must buy almost twice the rated amp-hours to get the same usable energy. That is why lithium usually wins on lifetime cost even at a higher price per kWh: you buy less capacity, it weighs a third as much, and it lasts several times longer before replacement.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Daily Energy Usage (kWh)
howToSteps.description: Add up the watt-hours your loads draw in a typical day — lights, fridge, pumps, laptops — and enter the total in kWh. Measured numbers from a battery monitor beat guesses.

howToSteps.title: Set Days of Autonomy and Depth of Discharge
howToSteps.description: Choose how many sunless days the bank must cover (1–2 for vans, 2–5 for cabins) and set depth of discharge to match your chemistry: about 80% for LiFePO4, 50% for lead-acid.

howToSteps.title: Pick System Voltage and Battery Type
howToSteps.description: Select 12 V, 24 V, or 48 V — higher voltage means lower current and thinner wiring — then choose LiFePO4, AGM, or flooded lead-acid to price the bank.

howToSteps.title: Calculate and Review
howToSteps.description: Hit Calculate to see total capacity, amp-hours, estimated cost, and cycle life. Re-run with a different voltage or chemistry to compare designs side by side.

tips: Measure daily usage with a battery monitor or shunt for a week rather than estimating — off-grid loads are almost always higher than people expect.
Match depth of discharge to your chemistry: entering 80% for a lead-acid bank will badly undersize it, since lead-acid should stop near 50%.
Add about 20% on top of the calculated capacity to cover inverter and wiring losses and give the bank room to grow into new loads.
Prefer 48 V for whole-home cabins and 12 V for small vans — higher voltage cuts amperage, wiring cost, and heat for the same power.
Compare lithium and lead-acid on usable amp-hours, not sticker price: lead-acid needs roughly double the rated capacity to match LiFePO4.
